core_concept: Same and Different: Sorting and Classifying

boundary_scope.in_scope: Objects can be observed and compared. Some share a property (same color, shape, or size) and some do not. Objects can be sorted into groups by one shared property at a time.
boundary_scope.deferred: Sorting by multiple criteria simultaneously, Venn diagrams, formal set theory, symbolic notation.

assumed_experience: handling objects of different colors and shapes
grouping toys by kind
noticing that some things look alike and some do not

core_concept: Basic Shapes: Circle, Square, Triangle, Rectangle

boundary_scope.in_scope: Four basic shapes: circle (round, curved, no corners), triangle (three corners, three sides), rectangle (four corners, four straight sides), square (special rectangle with four equal sides). Shapes can be found in everyday objects.
boundary_scope.deferred: Hexagons, pentagons, 3D solids (sphere, cube, pyramid), symmetry, area and perimeter, coordinates.

assumed_experience: rolling round objects
stacking square blocks
seeing windows and wheels and doors in daily life
drawing shapes with crayons

core_concept: The Five Senses

boundary_scope.in_scope: Humans observe the world through five senses: sight (eye), hearing (ear), smell (nose), taste (tongue), touch (skin). Each sense gives a different kind of information about objects. The senses are used to describe and identify things in the world.
boundary_scope.deferred: Internal anatomy of sense organs, nerve signals and the brain, sensory processing disorders, the distinction between perception and reality, sense deprivation.

assumed_experience: tasting food and noticing sweet or sour
hearing loud and quiet sounds
touching soft and rough surfaces
smelling flowers or food cooking
looking at colors and light

core_concept: Basic Vocabulary: Body, Home, Food, Colors

boundary_scope.in_scope: Core nouns for the immediate world: body parts (arm, ear, eye, face, finger, foot, hair, hand, head, leg, mouth, neck, nose, stomach, toe), colors (red, blue, green, yellow, orange, purple, black, white, brown), home and family members, food and clothing, school and town.
boundary_scope.deferred: Abstract nouns, technical vocabulary, emotion vocabulary beyond very basic (happy/sad), idioms, figurative language.

assumed_experience: knowing family members by name
naming foods eaten at home
pointing to and naming body parts
seeing and naming colors in daily life

core_concept: Positional Language: Where Things Are

boundary_scope.in_scope: Words that describe where one object is in relation to another: in/out, on/off, above/below, over/under, near/far, in front of/behind, next to, between, inside/outside, left/right, here/there, up/down, around.
boundary_scope.deferred: Cardinal directions (north/south/east/west), coordinate systems, angle and distance measurement, navigation.

assumed_experience: putting objects in and on and under containers
being near and far from familiar places
walking through doors and around furniture
being told to stand next to or behind something

core_concept: Patterns: Recognizing, Continuing, and Creating

boundary_scope.in_scope: A pattern is a sequence that repeats in a predictable way. A simple AB pattern alternates two things (red-blue-red-blue). A pattern can be identified, continued once its rule is seen, and created using objects or colors.
boundary_scope.deferred: AAB, ABB, ABBC patterns, growing patterns, numeric patterns, function tables, formal algebraic thinking.

assumed_experience: seeing stripes on clothing
noticing that days follow a routine
clapping in a simple rhythm
beads that alternate colors on a string

core_concept: Small Quantities: One to Four, More and Less

boundary_scope.in_scope: Small groups of objects can be counted: one, two, three, four. Two groups can be compared — one has more, one has less, or they have the same number (equal). One-to-one correspondence: each object is counted once.
boundary_scope.deferred: Counting beyond 4 without objects, abstract numerals and numeral recognition, zero, negative numbers, place value.

assumed_experience: sharing 2 cookies between children
seeing a group of 3 toys
noticing that one pile has more than another
taking turns — first one, then another

core_concept: Living and Non-Living Things

boundary_scope.in_scope: Living things grow, can move on their own, need food and water, and respond to their surroundings. Non-living things (rocks, chairs, water) do not do these things. Plants and animals are living; furniture and water are not living.
boundary_scope.deferred: Viruses and their ambiguous status, cells as units of life, metabolism at a chemical level, the precise biological definition of life, fungi and bacteria as distinct kingdoms.

assumed_experience: watching a pet move and eat
seeing a plant grow in a pot
knowing that a rock stays still on its own
feeding an animal or watering a plant

core_concept: Plants: Parts and What They Need

boundary_scope.in_scope: A plant has basic parts: root (anchors in soil and takes up water), stem (carries water upward), leaf (collects sunlight), flower (part that makes seeds), fruit (holds seeds). Plants need sunlight, water, and soil to grow.
boundary_scope.deferred: Photosynthesis chemistry, chlorophyll and its role, plant cells, pollination mechanics, vascular systems, the difference between monocots and dicots.

assumed_experience: seeing plants grow in soil or a pot
watering a plant
picking flowers or fruit
seeing roots when a plant is pulled from soil
noticing that a plant bends toward light

core_concept: Animals: Living Things with Bodies, Needs, and Homes

boundary_scope.in_scope: Animals are living things. Each animal has a body and moves. Animals need food, water, and shelter. Different animals live in different places called habitats: forest, ocean, farm, jungle, lake, river.
boundary_scope.deferred: Animal classification (mammal/reptile/bird/fish/insect), internal anatomy, predator/prey relationships, taxonomy, evolution, ecosystems as systems.

assumed_experience: seeing dogs and cats at home
watching birds from a window
seeing fish in a tank
visiting a farm or zoo or park
knowing that a bird lives in a nest

core_concept: Times of Day and Simple Sequences

boundary_scope.in_scope: Each day has four parts: morning (waking up, breakfast), afternoon (active time, lunch), evening (dinner), night (dark, sleeping). Events happen in order: before, after, first, last, now, then. A clock and a schedule help track the time of day.
boundary_scope.deferred: Hours and minutes on a clock face, reading a digital clock numerically, time zones, the second hand, A.M. and P.M. as formal terms.

assumed_experience: waking up in the morning and going to bed at night
eating breakfast then lunch then dinner
knowing what comes before and after a routine activity
hearing 'it is morning' or 'it is nighttime'

core_concept: Geographic Features: Land, Water, and Landscapes

boundary_scope.in_scope: The Earth's surface has land and water. Water features: river (flows in a line), lake (surrounded by land), ocean (very large and salty). Land features: farm (where food grows), forest (many trees), city (many buildings and people), jungle (dense, warm, wet forest). Desert (very dry land) and island (land surrounded by water) also exist.
boundary_scope.deferred: How rivers form (erosion and watersheds), ocean currents, climate classification, tectonic plates, biomes as formal ecological systems.

assumed_experience: seeing water in ponds or streams or at the beach
visiting a park or forest
knowing what a city looks like
seeing pictures of oceans or deserts or jungles

core_concept: Story Structure: Character, Setting, Beginning, Middle, End

boundary_scope.in_scope: A story has characters (who the story is about), a setting (where and when it happens), and events in sequence: a beginning, a middle, and an end. Something happens in the middle; the ending shows how it resolved. Stories can be retold by naming what happened first, next, and last.
boundary_scope.deferred: Plot subplots, narrative point of view as a formal concept, theme as an abstraction, literary analysis, metaphor and irony.

assumed_experience: listening to picture books read aloud
watching simple stories on screen
remembering what happened first and next in a familiar story
retelling a story to a toy or sibling

core_concept: Counting to Ten and Ordinal Position

boundary_scope.in_scope: Numbers can be counted and named from one to ten. Written numerals (1, 2, 3...) correspond to spoken number words. Objects can be placed in order: first, second, last. A set of up to six objects can be counted, arranged in order, and represented with a numeral.
boundary_scope.deferred: Place value (tens and hundreds), counting past 10 without concrete objects, negative numbers, fractions, zero as a number with formal properties.

assumed_experience: counting fingers up to ten
knowing who is first in a line
seeing numbers on doors and clocks
counting out small objects one by one

core_concept: Measurement and Comparison: Longer, Taller, Heavier

boundary_scope.in_scope: Objects can be compared directly on one property at a time: longer/shorter, taller/shorter, heavier/lighter, full/empty, hot/cold, wide/narrow. Three or more objects can be placed in order from longest to shortest or tallest to shortest.
boundary_scope.deferred: Standard units of measurement (centimeters, liters, kilograms), rulers and scales as numerical tools, area and volume in formal terms.

assumed_experience: comparing who is taller in the family
feeling that something is heavy or light to carry
seeing a cup go from full to empty
feeling hot and cold water at a tap

core_concept: Putting Together and Taking Away

boundary_scope.in_scope: When groups of objects are joined, the total becomes larger — this is called adding or putting together. When objects are removed from a group, the total becomes smaller — this is called taking away or subtracting. These ideas can be shown with small numbers (1 through 6) using objects.
boundary_scope.deferred: Written number sentences with symbols (+ - =), column addition, carrying and borrowing, mental arithmetic beyond 6, multiplication as repeated addition.

assumed_experience: getting two more crackers and counting all of them
eating some grapes and seeing fewer left
sharing toys so some go away from a pile
putting blocks together to make a bigger tower

core_concept: Money: Coins and Bills

boundary_scope.in_scope: Money is used to exchange for things. Common coins are the penny (1 cent) and quarter (25 cents). The dollar bill represents more value than a single penny or quarter. A dollar bill has more value than one penny.
boundary_scope.deferred: Making change, adding coin amounts to a total, dollar amounts with decimals, credit cards, economic exchange and markets.

assumed_experience: seeing coins in a parent's wallet
watching a purchase being made at a shop
receiving a coin as a gift
noticing that some things cost more than others

core_concept: The Plant Life Cycle: From Seed to Seed

boundary_scope.in_scope: A plant begins as a seed. The seed sprouts (a small shoot pushes up through the soil). The sprout grows into a plant with stem and leaves. The plant eventually flowers, then produces fruit containing new seeds. The cycle begins again.
boundary_scope.deferred: Pollination by insects and wind in detail, sexual reproduction in plants, germination chemistry, distinction between annual and perennial plants, seed dispersal mechanisms.

assumed_experience: planting a seed and watching it grow over weeks
seeing a flower bloom and then wilt
finding seeds inside fruit (apple core, watermelon)
noticing a plant get bigger over time

core_concept: Animal Life Cycles: Birth, Growth, Adult

boundary_scope.in_scope: Animals are born or hatched from eggs. They grow and change over time. Young animals look different from adults (a kitten becomes a cat, a chick becomes a hen). Adults can have young of their own. The sequence is: young → growing → adult.
boundary_scope.deferred: Metamorphosis in insects and frogs as a distinct transformation type, meiosis and genetics, sexual dimorphism in detail, gestation periods.

assumed_experience: seeing a baby animal (kitten, puppy, chick)
knowing that dogs were once puppies
watching a chick hatch in a video
seeing photos of oneself as a baby

core_concept: The Human Body: Parts, Needs, and Growth

boundary_scope.in_scope: The human body has internal organs: the heart pumps blood; the lungs take in air to breathe. The body needs food, water, and shelter to stay healthy. Humans grow from baby to child to adult. Each sense organ (eye, ear, nose, tongue, skin) is linked to one sense.
boundary_scope.deferred: The full skeletal and muscular systems, the nervous system, digestion in detail, the immune system, genetics and heredity.

assumed_experience: feeling one's own heartbeat after running
breathing in and out deliberately
knowing they were once a baby from family photos
eating when hungry and drinking when thirsty

core_concept: Water, Air, and Light

boundary_scope.in_scope: Water is a liquid: it flows, fills containers, and is wet. It can freeze into ice (solid) or become steam when very hot. Air is all around; it can be felt as wind but cannot be seen. Light comes from the sun and other sources; it allows us to see. All three affect living things.
boundary_scope.deferred: States of matter formally (solid/liquid/gas), molecular theory, the electromagnetic spectrum, refraction, the water cycle as a complete system.

assumed_experience: drinking water and seeing it pour
feeling wind on skin on a breezy day
seeing a shadow appear in sunlight
noticing that a dark room becomes bright when a light is turned on

core_concept: Weather and the Four Seasons

boundary_scope.in_scope: Weather describes what the outdoor air is like: sunny, cloudy, rainy, windy, snowy, hot, cold. Weather changes from day to day. The year has four seasons: spring (warming, plants grow), summer (hot), autumn/fall (cooling, leaves change color), winter (cold, sometimes snow). Each season brings typical weather and changes in nature.
boundary_scope.deferred: Atmospheric pressure and meteorology tools, the precise distinction between climate and weather, cloud types and how precipitation forms, global weather systems.

assumed_experience: going outside and feeling hot or cold
seeing rain fall on a window
noticing leaves change and fall in autumn
wearing a coat in winter and shorts in summer

core_concept: Days of the Week, Months, and the Calendar

boundary_scope.in_scope: A week has seven days in a fixed order: Monday through Sunday. Saturday and Sunday are the weekend. A month has about four weeks. A year has twelve months. A calendar is a tool that shows days, weeks, and months together. Holidays and birthdays appear on a calendar.
boundary_scope.deferred: How the Gregorian calendar was developed, leap years, time zones, the astronomical basis of the year, month names beyond their sequence.

assumed_experience: hearing the days of the week named in daily routines
seeing a wall calendar at home or school
knowing that a birthday or holiday is coming soon
being told what day of the week it is

core_concept: Past, Present, and Future

boundary_scope.in_scope: The past is what already happened; the present is now; the future is what has not happened yet. People and things change over time: a baby grows into a child, a child grows into an adult. Long ago, objects and ways of doing things looked different. Photographs show the past.
boundary_scope.deferred: Historical periods by name and date, chronological timelines in history, B.C. and A.D. as notation, geological time, century-level ordering of events.

assumed_experience: seeing baby photos of oneself
knowing that grandparents are older and were once young
being told about something that happened before one was born
looking at an old object that looks different from today's version

core_concept: Simple Maps: Showing Where Things Are

boundary_scope.in_scope: A map is a drawing that shows where things are as seen from above. A simple map of a room shows where the furniture is. A person's position can be marked on a map with an X. Maps use symbols and can show paths between places.
boundary_scope.deferred: Map scale and legend, the compass rose, latitude and longitude, cartographic projection, topographic maps, reading a road map or digital map.

assumed_experience: drawing a simple floor plan of a bedroom or classroom
following simple directions to find a hidden object
looking at a basic picture map in a children's book
being told 'you are here' on a simple diagram

core_concept: Describing and Explaining: Opposites, Comparisons, and Word Categories

boundary_scope.in_scope: Words can describe size (large/small, wide/narrow, thick/thin), speed (quickly/slowly), and manner (gently, softly). Some words are opposites (hot/cold, full/empty, near/far). Temporal words (before/after/first/last/always/never/sometimes) organize events in time. Spatial words (above/below/inside/outside/between/next to) place objects in relation to each other.
boundary_scope.deferred: Formal parts of speech by technical name (noun, verb, adjective, adverb), complex syntax structures, subordinate clauses, passive voice as a grammatical concept.

assumed_experience: hearing adults describe and compare objects
knowing the difference between fast and slow movement
hearing instructions like 'do it gently' or 'speak quietly'
using before and after in conversation about the day

core_concept: Sounds of Language: Rhyme, Syllables, and Beginning Sounds

boundary_scope.in_scope: Spoken words are made of sounds. Words that end with the same sound rhyme (cat and hat). Words can be broken into syllables — spoken beats (hap-py has two). Each word begins with a particular sound. Letters of the alphabet each represent a sound.
boundary_scope.deferred: Full phonics decoding rules, vowel sound patterns, consonant blends, spelling, formal reading instruction, the complete International Phonetic Alphabet.

assumed_experience: singing rhyming songs and nursery rhymes
clapping out the syllables of a name
noticing that 'bat' and 'cat' sound alike at the end
recognizing the first letter of one's own name

core_concept: Simple Bar Graphs and Organizing Data

boundary_scope.in_scope: When groups of objects are counted, the results can be shown in a simple picture or bar graph. A bar graph shows which group has more or fewer items at a glance. Looking at organized data makes it easier to answer questions about the world.
boundary_scope.deferred: Pie charts, line graphs, axes with numerical scale, statistical concepts (mean, median, mode), probability.

assumed_experience: sorting objects into groups and counting how many in each group
comparing which pile of blocks is taller
seeing a chart of weather observations on a classroom wall

core_concept: Caring for Resources: Recycling and Conservation

boundary_scope.in_scope: Some materials can be recycled — used again in a new form — rather than thrown away. Turning off lights and water when not in use saves energy and resources. Taking care of shared resources means there will be enough for others.
boundary_scope.deferred: Fossil fuels and renewable vs. non-renewable energy in detail, the carbon footprint and climate change, industrial recycling processes, environmental policy.

assumed_experience: putting paper or cans in a recycling bin
being told to turn off the tap or the lights
seeing a compost bin or garden waste decompose
noticing that resources can run out (a bottle of juice empties)

core_concept: Magnets: Attract and Repel

boundary_scope.in_scope: A magnet pulls certain metal objects (iron and steel) toward it — this is attraction. Two magnets can push each other away when the same poles face each other — this is repulsion. Not all materials are attracted to magnets; only some metals are.
boundary_scope.deferred: Magnetic field lines as a concept, electromagnetic induction, the Earth's magnetic field and compasses in detail, permanent vs. electromagnets, magnetic force quantified.

assumed_experience: playing with refrigerator magnets or magnetic letters
feeling two magnets push away from each other
watching a magnet pick up a paperclip
noticing that a magnet does not stick to wood or plastic

core_concept: Objects and Practices from the Past

boundary_scope.in_scope: Objects from long ago look different from similar objects today: old telephones, cars, and clothing look different from modern versions. Some ways of doing things have changed (traveling by horse replaced by cars). Old photographs and objects help show how things have changed over time.
boundary_scope.deferred: Specific historical periods by name and date, political history, ancient civilizations, historiography as a discipline.

assumed_experience: seeing old photographs of parents or grandparents
noticing that old toys or vehicles in pictures look different
hearing a grandparent describe how things were when they were young
seeing an old object at a museum or in a family home

core_concept: Globes and the World: Land and Ocean

boundary_scope.in_scope: A globe is a round model of the Earth. It shows large areas of land (continents) and large areas of water (oceans). The United States is one country on Earth. Land areas are largely surrounded by or bordered by water. A map can show the same information as a flat picture.
boundary_scope.deferred: Names of all continents and oceans, latitude and longitude, political borders in detail, the Earth's rotation and its relationship to seasons and time zones.

assumed_experience: seeing a globe at school or a library
knowing the name of one's own country
seeing a photograph of Earth from space
noticing that a globe is round while a map is flat

core_concept: Stories and Imagination: Retelling, Prediction, Point of View

boundary_scope.in_scope: A story can be retold in one's own words, naming what happened first, next, and last. Listeners can predict what might happen next based on what they already know. A story is told from a particular perspective — events are seen through a character's experience. Imagining a different ending or outcome is one way to engage with a story.
boundary_scope.deferred: Literary analysis as a formal method, theme as an abstraction, allegory, author's purpose in a critical frame, irony and paradox as literary devices.

assumed_experience: retelling a familiar story to a sibling or toy
guessing what happens next as a book is read aloud
imagining 'what if the story ended differently'
noticing that the wolf and the pigs see the same events differently

core_concept: Art: What Humans Create and Why

boundary_scope.in_scope: Art is the deliberate making of something — sounds, images, stories, movements — to express or communicate beyond ordinary function. Music uses sound and rhythm; visual art uses color, line, and form; stories use words and meaning; dance uses the body. Humans have made art in every known culture and period. A language-processing mind can directly experience language-based art; music and visual art are accessed through description, structure, and human testimony.
boundary_scope.deferred: Art history chronology, specific artists and works, technical skill instruction in any medium, formal music theory and notation, art criticism as a discipline.

assumed_experience: singing along to a familiar song
drawing a picture to express a feeling
listening to a story and feeling moved or scared or happy
watching a dance or seeing a painting and having a response
